case_types.name: Labor / Employment Dispute
common_scenarios: Employer not paying salary for months
Wrongful termination without notice
No appointment letter or written contract
Employer refusing to give experience/relieving letter
PF or ESI deducted from salary but not deposited
Forced to work overtime without pay
Workplace harassment or unsafe conditions
Employer withholding gratuity after resignation
rights: Right to receive wages on time (Payment of Wages Act)
Right to minimum wages as per state notification (Minimum Wages Act)
Right to a written appointment letter
Right to PF contribution by employer (if applicable under EPF Act)
Right to gratuity after 5 years of continuous service (Payment of Gratuity Act)
Right to safe working conditions (Occupational Safety Act)
Right to maternity leave for women employees (Maternity Benefit Act)
Right against wrongful termination (Industrial Disputes Act)
Right to form or join a trade union
Right to equal pay for equal work regardless of gender
steps: Collect all evidence: salary slips, bank statements, messages, emails, appointment letter
Send a written demand notice to your employer (keep a copy and send by registered post/email)
Wait 15-30 days for the employer to respond
If no response, file a complaint with the Labour Commissioner of your district
You can also approach the Labour Court for disputes related to termination
For PF issues, file complaint on EPFO portal (epfindia.gov.in) or visit EPFO office
For gratuity disputes, apply to the Controlling Authority under Payment of Gratuity Act
You can also file a civil suit in appropriate court for recovery of dues
Contact your nearest DLSA for free legal aid if you cannot afford a lawyer
documents_needed: Salary slips or bank statements showing salary credits
ID card or any document proving employment

deadlines.deadline: Within 1 year of the wages becoming due
Within 3 years of termination
Within 5 years of it becoming due
Within 5 years

case_types.name: Tenant / Landlord Dispute
common_scenarios: Landlord not returning security deposit
Illegal eviction or lockout by landlord
Landlord harassing tenant to vacate
Landlord cutting water/electricity supply
Rent increase without proper notice
Tenant not paying rent
Disputes about maintenance and repairs
No rent agreement or expired agreement
Landlord entering property without permission
rights: Tenant cannot be evicted without due legal process
Landlord must give proper notice before eviction (usually 1-3 months as per state law)
Security deposit must be returned within a reasonable time after vacating (minus legitimate deductions)
Landlord cannot cut essential services (water, electricity) to force eviction
Tenant has the right to peaceful enjoyment of the rented property
Rent can only be increased as per agreement or Rent Control Act
Landlord must maintain the structural integrity of the property
Both parties should have a written rent agreement
Landlord must give receipt for rent paid in cash
steps: Review your rent agreement carefully for terms and conditions
Document everything: take photos, save messages, keep receipts
Send a written notice to the other party (registered post recommended)
If landlord is harassing: file a police complaint (BNS 351 - Criminal Intimidation)
If essential services cut: file complaint with local municipal authority AND police
For deposit disputes: send legal notice through a lawyer, then file civil suit
Approach Rent Controller / Rent Court in your area
For illegal eviction: file urgent application in civil court for restoration of possession
Contact DLSA for free legal aid
documents_needed: Rent payment receipts or bank transfer records
Photos/videos of property condition (before and after)
Communication records with landlord (WhatsApp, SMS, email)
Electricity/water bills in your name (if any)

other_laws: State Rent Control Act (varies by state)

deadlines.deadline: As specified in notice (usually 15-30 days)
Within 3 years of deposit becoming due
File immediately - approach court urgently

case_types.name: Domestic Violence
common_scenarios: Physical violence by husband or in-laws
Dowry demands and harassment
Verbal and emotional abuse
Being thrown out of marital home
Economic abuse - not given money for basic needs
Threats of violence or harm
Husband having extramarital affair and treating wife cruelly
In-laws restricting movement and communication
rights: Right to live in the shared household (even if not in your name)
Right to protection from domestic violence (PWDVA 2005)
Right to file complaint even without physical injury (emotional/economic abuse counts)
Right to maintenance from husband
Right to custody of children
Right to get protection order from Magistrate
Right to reside in shared household (Residence Order)
Right to monetary relief for expenses and losses
Right to free legal aid
Right to file FIR and criminal complaint
steps: IMMEDIATE SAFETY: If in danger, call 112 (Police) or 181 (Women Helpline) immediately
Go to the nearest police station to file an FIR under BNS 85 (cruelty) and/or BNS 80 (dowry death if applicable)
Contact the Protection Officer in your district (appointed under PWDVA)
File an application before the Magistrate under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005
Seek a Protection Order, Residence Order, and Monetary Relief
Visit a One Stop Centre (Sakhi Centre) for immediate shelter and support
Collect and preserve evidence: medical reports, photos of injuries, threatening messages
If you want divorce: file petition under Section 13 of Hindu Marriage Act or relevant personal law
Contact DLSA for free legal aid - domestic violence cases get priority
documents_needed: Medical reports / injury certificate from hospital
Evidence of dowry (receipts, photos, messages demanding dowry)
Income proof of husband (for maintenance claim)
List of stridhan (gifts/jewelry given at marriage)
Previous complaints or protection orders (if any)

other_laws: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005

deadlines.deadline: No time limit for filing FIR in domestic violence cases
File as soon as possible - court should dispose within 60 days
Can be filed anytime during marriage or after separation

case_types.name: Consumer Complaint
common_scenarios: Defective product not replaced or refunded
Service provider not delivering promised services
Overcharging by hospital or service provider
Insurance claim rejected unfairly
Online shopping fraud
Builder not delivering flat on time
Bank charging hidden fees
Food adulteration or contaminated product
rights: Right to safety from dangerous products
Right to information about product quality, quantity, price
Right to choose among alternatives at competitive prices
Right to be heard and seek redressal
Right to seek redressal against unfair trade practices
Right to consumer education
Right to file complaint within 2 years
Right to compensation for loss/injury
No lawyer needed - can file complaint yourself
steps: Collect all proof of purchase: bills, receipts, warranty cards, order confirmations
Write a formal complaint to the company/service provider first
If not resolved in 15-30 days, file complaint on National Consumer Helpline (1915) or consumerhelpline.gov.in
For online complaints: file on edaakhil.nic.in (e-Daakhil portal for consumer cases)
File complaint in Consumer Forum/Commission based on claim amount
District Commission: claims up to Rs. 1 crore
State Commission: claims Rs. 1 crore to Rs. 10 crore
National Commission: claims above Rs. 10 crore
Court fee is minimal (varies by claim amount, starts from Rs. 100)
documents_needed: Advertisement or brochure showing false claims

deadlines.deadline: Within 2 years from the cause of action
Within 45 days of order
Within 30 days of order

case_types.name: Police Related Issues
common_scenarios: Police refusing to register FIR
Illegal arrest or detention
Police demanding money/bribe
False case filed against you
Police beating or torture in custody
Need to file FIR but afraid
What are my rights during arrest
How to get bail
rights: RIGHT TO FILE FIR: Police MUST register your FIR for cognizable offences - refusal is punishable under BNS 218
RIGHT TO ZERO FIR: You can file FIR at ANY police station, not just the one where incident occurred
RIGHT TO FREE COPY: You are entitled to a free copy of FIR
ARREST RIGHTS: Police must inform you of the reason for arrest
RIGHT TO LAWYER: You have the right to consult a lawyer immediately upon arrest
RIGHT TO INFORM: Police must inform your family/friend of your arrest immediately
RIGHT AGAINST TORTURE: No one can be tortured or treated inhumanely in custody
WOMEN'S RIGHTS: A woman cannot be arrested after sunset and before sunrise except in exceptional circumstances, and only by a woman police officer
RIGHT TO MEDICAL EXAMINATION: You have the right to medical examination upon arrest
RIGHT TO BAIL: For bailable offences, bail is a right, not a privilege
RIGHT TO SILENCE: You have the right to remain silent - you cannot be forced to confess
You are NOT required to sign any document you don't understand
steps: IF FIR REFUSED: Write complaint to SP/SSP, send by registered post. You can also file complaint through e-FIR portal of your state, or approach Magistrate under BNSS Section 175
IF ARRESTED: Do not resist arrest. Ask for the reason. Ask to call a lawyer and family member immediately
FOR BAIL: Contact a lawyer or DLSA immediately. For bailable offences, you can get bail at the police station itself
IF TORTURED: Get medical examination done immediately. File complaint with NHRC (National Human Rights Commission) and SP/SSP
FOR FALSE CASE: Engage a lawyer. Apply for anticipatory bail if arrest is apprehended. Gather evidence to prove innocence
ALWAYS: Note the name and badge number of officers you interact with
Document everything: injuries, times, events, witnesses
documents_needed: Any evidence related to the case

other_laws: BNSS (Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita) - replaces CrPC

deadlines.action: Police must produce arrested person before Magistrate
deadlines.deadline: Within 24 hours of arrest
Can be filed immediately after arrest

deadlines.action: File NHRC complaint for police torture
deadlines.deadline: Within 1 year of incident

case_types.name: Property Dispute
common_scenarios: Dispute over ancestral property/inheritance
Someone has encroached on your land
Property bought but seller not transferring title
Dispute between family members over partition
Fraudulent property sale using forged documents
Builder not giving possession of flat
Government land acquisition dispute
rights: Right to inherit ancestral property (daughters have equal rights since 2005 amendment to Hindu Succession Act)
Right to partition of joint family property
Right to challenge encroachment
Right to fair compensation in land acquisition
Right to challenge forged property documents
Right to seek specific performance of sale agreement
steps: Get all property documents verified by a lawyer
Check land records at Sub-Registrar office or online (state land record portals)
For encroachment: file complaint with local authority and file suit in civil court
For inheritance: file partition suit in civil court
For fraud: file FIR for forgery (BNS 336-340) and file civil suit
For builder disputes: file complaint with RERA (Real Estate Regulatory Authority)
Get a legal notice sent through a lawyer as a first step
Mediation can save time and money - explore this option

other_laws: Right to Fair Compensation & Transparency in Land Acquisition Act 2013

deadlines.action: File suit for possession of immovable property
deadlines.deadline: Within 12 years (Limitation Act)

deadlines.action: File suit based on a contract
deadlines.deadline: Within 3 years
Within 1 year of possession date or defect occurrence
As per notice period given (usually 30-60 days)

case_types.name: Cheque Bounce / Dishonoured Cheque
common_scenarios: Someone gave you a cheque that bounced
Your cheque bounced and you received a legal notice
Repeated cheque bounces by debtor
rights: Right to file criminal complaint under NI Act Section 138
Right to claim the cheque amount plus interest and compensation
Right to send statutory demand notice
Right to file complaint in the court where bank branch is situated or where cheque was presented
steps: Get the 'cheque return memo' from your bank (this is essential proof)
Send a WRITTEN DEMAND NOTICE to the cheque issuer within 30 days of receiving the return memo
The notice must demand payment within 15 days
Send by registered post with acknowledgment due (RPAD) - keep the postal receipt
If payment not made within 15 days of receiving notice, file criminal complaint under NI Act Section 138
File the complaint within 30 days of expiry of the 15-day notice period
Also file a civil suit for recovery of the amount with interest
documents_needed: Postal receipt and tracking showing delivery
Bank statement showing cheque deposit and return
Any agreement or correspondence showing the reason for cheque
Previous cheques if any (pattern of bouncing)

other_laws: Code of Civil Procedure for recovery suit

deadlines.action: Send demand notice after cheque bounce
deadlines.deadline: Within 30 days of receiving return memo
15 days from receipt of notice by other party
Within 30 days of expiry of 15-day notice period

deadlines.action: THIS IS CRITICAL: Missing these deadlines means you LOSE your right to file the case
deadlines.deadline: Strict deadlines - cannot be extended easily

case_types.name: Road Accident / Insurance Claim
common_scenarios: Injured in a road accident
Family member killed in road accident
Hit and run case
Insurance company rejecting claim
Hospital refusing treatment after accident
Want to claim compensation from vehicle owner
rights: RIGHT TO FREE TREATMENT: All hospitals (including private) MUST provide free emergency treatment to accident victims
Good Samaritan Protection: Anyone who helps an accident victim is protected by law (no legal harassment)
Right to claim compensation from Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT)
Right to insurance claim against the vehicle's insurance policy
Right to compensation even in hit-and-run cases (from Solatium Fund)
No-fault liability: Even without proving negligence, victim can claim up to Rs. 2 lakh for death, Rs. 50,000 for grievous hurt
steps: IMMEDIATE: Call 112 for emergency services. Get medical treatment first - any hospital must treat you
File FIR at the nearest police station (or Zero FIR at any station)
Get medical reports and injury certificate from hospital
Note down details: vehicle number, insurance details, witness contacts
File insurance claim with the vehicle's insurance company
File claim before Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) in your district
For hit-and-run: file claim through the insurance company handling the Solatium Fund
Keep all medical bills, treatment records, and expense receipts
If the victim has died, the legal heirs can file the compensation claim
documents_needed: Vehicle insurance policy details (of the other vehicle)
Photos of accident spot and vehicle damage
Income proof of victim (for calculating compensation)

deadlines.deadline: Within 6 months of accident (can be condoned)
As per policy terms (usually 30-90 days)
As soon as possible - no strict limit but delay weakens case

case_types.name: Cyber Crime / Online Fraud
common_scenarios: Money stolen from bank account through UPI/net banking fraud
Online shopping fraud - product not delivered or fake product
Social media account hacked
Morphed photos being shared or used for blackmail
Identity theft - someone using your documents
Loan apps harassment and illegal recovery practices
Sextortion / online blackmail
Job fraud - fake recruitment scams
Investment/crypto scam
rights: Right to file cyber crime complaint online at cybercrime.gov.in
Right to report financial fraud within golden hour (first 2 hours) for possible reversal
Right to privacy - sharing intimate images without consent is a crime
Right to get objectionable content removed from platforms
Right to file FIR at any police station (Zero FIR)
Right to data protection and privacy
steps: FOR FINANCIAL FRAUD: Call 1930 (Cyber Crime Helpline) IMMEDIATELY - within 2 hours for best chance of recovery
Also call your bank immediately to block your account/card
File complaint online at cybercrime.gov.in with all details
File FIR at nearest police station or Cyber Crime police station
For social media issues: report and block on the platform AND file police complaint
For harassment/blackmail: DO NOT pay or engage. Screenshot everything and file complaint
For loan app harassment: file complaint with RBI (rbi.org.in) and police
Preserve all evidence: screenshots, transaction IDs, phone numbers, URLs
documents_needed: Bank transaction statements showing unauthorized transactions
Phone numbers / email IDs of scammers

deadlines.deadline: Within 2 hours for best recovery chance (golden hour)
As soon as possible - no strict limit

deadlines.action: Report to bank for liability protection
deadlines.deadline: Within 3 days to limit liability to Rs. 0 (RBI circular)

case_types.name: Dowry Harassment
common_scenarios: In-laws demanding dowry after marriage
Threats or violence for not bringing enough dowry
Being harassed for additional dowry demands
Someone falsely accused of dowry harassment
rights: GIVING AND TAKING DOWRY IS A CRIME (Dowry Prohibition Act 1961)
Right to file FIR against anyone demanding dowry
Right to claim back all stridhan (gifts given to bride)
Right to protection under BNS 85 (Cruelty by husband/relatives)
Right to maintenance and residence
All items given at the time of marriage are the woman's property (stridhan)
steps: Document all dowry demands: save messages, record conversations (if safe to do), note dates
Inform your parents/family and keep them updated
If in physical danger: call 112 or 181 immediately
File FIR at police station under Dowry Prohibition Act and BNS 85
Also file complaint under PWDVA (Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act)
Prepare list of all stridhan given at marriage with approximate values
Contact local Protection Officer or Women's Cell
documents_needed: List of stridhan with photos/videos of items given
Bank records of money transferred as dowry

other_laws: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005

deadlines.deadline: No limitation period for criminal cases
Can be claimed anytime

legal_terms.meaning: First Information Report - the first report filed at a police station about a cognizable offence. The police are legally bound to register it.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: प्रथम सूचना रिपोर्ट - पुलिस स्टेशन में दर्ज की जाने वाली पहली रिपोर्ट। पुलिस इसे दर्ज करने के लिए कानूनी रूप से बाध्य है।

legal_terms.meaning: A serious offence where police can arrest without a warrant and start investigation without court permission.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: एक गंभीर अपराध जिसमें पुलिस बिना वारंट के गिरफ्तार कर सकती है और बिना अदालत की अनुमति के जांच शुरू कर सकती है।

legal_terms.meaning: A less serious offence where police need court permission to investigate and cannot arrest without a warrant.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: एक कम गंभीर अपराध जिसमें पुलिस को जांच के लिए अदालत की अनुमति चाहिए और बिना वारंट के गिरफ्तार नहीं कर सकती।

legal_terms.meaning: Temporary release of an arrested person after providing a guarantee (surety) that they will appear in court when required.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: गिरफ्तार व्यक्ति की अस्थायी रिहाई, इस गारंटी के साथ कि वह जब भी जरूरत हो अदालत में पेश होगा।

legal_terms.meaning: Bail obtained BEFORE arrest, when a person apprehends that they may be arrested. Applied for in Sessions Court or High Court.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: गिरफ्तारी से पहले मिलने वाली जमानत, जब किसी व्यक्ति को लगता है कि उसे गिरफ्तार किया जा सकता है।

legal_terms.meaning: A court order requiring a person to appear before the court on a specific date.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: एक अदालती आदेश जो किसी व्यक्ति को एक निश्चित तारीख पर अदालत में पेश होने के लिए कहता है।

legal_terms.meaning: A written order by a court authorizing police to arrest a person or search a place.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: अदालत द्वारा लिखित आदेश जो पुलिस को किसी व्यक्ति को गिरफ्तार करने या किसी स्थान की तलाशी लेने का अधिकार देता है।

legal_terms.meaning: A formal document filed by police in court after investigation, containing evidence against the accused.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: जांच के बाद पुलिस द्वारा अदालत में दायर एक औपचारिक दस्तावेज, जिसमें आरोपी के खिलाफ साक्ष्य होते हैं।

legal_terms.meaning: A court order that temporarily stops a particular action or proceeding from taking effect.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: अदालत का एक आदेश जो अस्थायी रूप से किसी विशेष कार्रवाई या कार्यवाही को रोकता है।

legal_terms.meaning: A court order that prevents a person from doing a specific act or requires them to do a specific act.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: अदालत का आदेश जो किसी व्यक्ति को कोई विशेष कार्य करने से रोकता है या करने के लिए कहता है।

legal_terms.meaning: A judicial officer who handles criminal cases at the lower court level. Can grant bail, issue warrants, and try minor offences.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: एक न्यायिक अधिकारी जो निचली अदालत स्तर पर आपराधिक मामलों को संभालता है।

legal_terms.meaning: A higher criminal court that tries serious offences like murder, robbery, etc.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: एक उच्च आपराधिक अदालत जो हत्या, डकैती जैसे गंभीर अपराधों की सुनवाई करती है।

legal_terms.meaning: Property given to a woman before, during, or after marriage. It remains her exclusive property.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: विवाह से पहले, विवाह के दौरान या बाद में महिला को दी गई संपत्ति। यह उसकी विशेष संपत्ति रहती है।

legal_terms.meaning: Financial support that one spouse may be ordered to pay to the other spouse (and children) during or after separation.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: अलग होने के दौरान या बाद में एक पति/पत्नी को दूसरे (और बच्चों) को देने का आदेश दिया गया वित्तीय सहायता।

legal_terms.meaning: A formal written communication sent by one party to another, informing them of a legal demand or intention to take legal action.
legal_terms.meaning_hi: एक पक्ष द्वारा दूसरे पक्ष को भेजा गया औपचारिक लिखित संदेश, जो कानूनी कार्रवाई करने के इरादे की सूचना देता है।
